content:
{
  "title": "Function Signature Infilling: Structure-Aware Prompting for Code Generation",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate code with syntactic errors or incorrect function usage, particularly when prompt instructions are ambiguous or lack structural guidance.",
  "motivation": "Despite improvements in LLMs, their code generation remains brittle without explicit structural cues, leading to practical limitations for real-world adoption. Recent work shows that providing partial code context or emphasizing function signatures can guide LLMs towards better-structured outputs. Harnessing this structural bias could yield significant accuracy and reliability gains in code generation tasks.",
  "method": "We propose Function Signature Infilling (FSI) prompts: given a natural language task description, we prepend the prompt with a partial function signature (name, arguments, type hints) and a code skeleton with key control structures (e.g., for/if blocks) left as placeholders. The LLM is instructed to complete both the signature and the function body. By explicitly encoding target structure, FSI aligns model outputs with correct syntax and relevant API usage. Prompt templates are dynamically built from NL instructions using lightweight static analysis or retrieval from codebases. This method is model-agnostic and requires no gradient updates.",
  "experiment_plan": "Evaluate FSI prompting on HumanEval and MBPP datasets, comparing against standard NL-only prompts and chain-of-thought baselines. Metrics include pass@1, syntactic correctness, and API usage accuracy. Conduct ablations to assess the impact of signature and skeleton components separately."
}
